2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/88c7d2c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6720x3771+0+354/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fe4%2F25%2F781cf9fa4e7f8b2bf6cb5f65c666%2F20220602-melody-angel-mm00228.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Chicago’s own Melody Angel will open for Sheila E on June 22.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Indie-alternative sounds take over the Pavilion on June 29 with performances by <a class="Link" href="https://sirchloemusic.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Sir Chloe</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/venusandtheflytraps/" target="_blank" ><u>Venus & the Flytraps</u></a>. Chicago musician, pop culture critic and DJ <a class="Link" href="https://www.3arts.org/artist/jill-hopkins/" target="_blank" ><u>Jill Hopkins</u></a> will set the mood with an opening set.</p><p>After about two weeks off, the summer concerts pick up again on July 16 with an enchanting night of samba, jazz, funk and bossa nova from Brazil’s <a class="Link" href="https://open.spotify.com/artist/5I0EPnV9gwrZYTbScjnaOk" target="_blank" ><u>Marcos Valle</u></a> — the man often credited for popularizing the latter genre throughout the 1960s.</p><p>Drummer <a class="Link" href="https://danielvillarrealmusic.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Daniel Villarreal</u></a> and Chicago-based multimedia artist and vinyl dealer <a class="Link" href="https://www.clarknelson.net/about" target="_blank" ><u>Clark Nelson</u></a>, aka DJ Clark Quente, will also perform that night. <a class="Link" href="https://www.thebudos.com/" target="_blank" ><u>The Budos Band</u></a>, a funky Staten Island group specializing in “doom rock,” and <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/zeshan/" target="_blank" ><u>Zeshan B</u></a>, a Chicago native who sings in English, Urdu, and Punjabi, perform on Aug. 3. A five-person a cappella group from Chicago known as <a class="Link" href="https://www.radiusvocals.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Radius</u></a> opens.</p><p>The festival culminates on Aug. 6 with a night featuring South Side-born and bred rapper Matt B, who <a class="Link" href="https://www.grammy.com/series-or-collection/2025-grammys-67th-annual-grammy-awards-videos/matt-b-wins-best-global-music-album-2025-grammys" target="_blank" ><u>won a Grammy</u></a> for his studio album “Alkebulan II,” featuring London’s <a class="Link" href="https://www.rpo.co.uk/" target="_blank" ><u>Royal Philharmonic Orchestra</u></a>. That night also features forward-looking performances from rising stars <a class="Link" href="https://www.lilaikemusic.com/" target="_blank" ><u>Lila Iké</u></a>, a Jamaican singer-songwriter whose debut album “Treasure Self Love” was nominated for a Grammy in 2025, and <a class="Link" href="https://soundcloud.com/blairelisemusic" target="_blank" ><u>Blair Elise</u></a>, an up-and-coming multi-hyphenate artist from New Orleans.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/01/06/grant-park-festival-chicago-classical-millennium-park-music-free-ben-folds" target="_blank" ><u>The Grant Park Music Festival</u></a> previously announced its return this season on Wednesday, Friday and Saturday nights in Millennium Park starting June 10. The second season under Artistic Director and Principal Conductor <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2025/06/06/giancarlo-guerrero-grant-park-symphony-music-festival-conductor" target="_blank" ><u>Giancarlo Guerrero</u></a> will feature a heavy dose of music from American composers, as the country marks its 250th birthday. Highlights also include a performance by singer-songwriter Ben Folds, the one-time frontman of the rock trio Ben Folds Five, who makes his festival debut on July 29.</p><h3><b>Here’s the full 2026 Millennium Park summer music lineup:</b></h3><p></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="margin-top:0;margin-bottom:0;padding-inline-start:48px;" id="rte-a1b16d9b-495a-11f1-90ba-4789f25c2b09"><li><b>June 15</b>: Arrested Development, Linda Sol, DJ Ca$h Era</li><li><b>June 18</b>: Let the Spirit Out! A Jubilant Celebration of Juneteenth with D-Composed and Kahil El’Zabar’s Ethnic Heritage Ensemble, with special guests Nona Hendryx and David Murray</li><li><b>June 22</b>: Sheila E., Melody Angel, DJ Larz Natural</li><li><b>June 25</b>: Patrice Rushen, Sparklmami, DJ Shon Dervis</li><li><b>June 29</b>: Sir Chloe, Venus & the Flytraps, DJ Jill Hopkins</li><li><b>July 16</b>: Marcos Valle, Daniel Villarreal, DJ Clark Quente</li><li><b>July 27</b>: Aterciopelados, La Rosa Noir, Lumpen Radio DJs</li><li><b>Aug. 3</b>: The Budos Band, Zeshan B, Radius</li><li><b>Aug. 6</b>: GRAMMY Legacies and Looking Ahead feat. Lila Ike, Matt B, DJ Blair Elise</li></ul><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-8f0000" name="module-8f0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><b>More summer fun at Millennium Park:</b></h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-966ae4d2-495d-11f1-ab4d-ef275086174d"><li>A Rob Reiner double feature, the Oscar-winning “Sinners” and the movie version of the Lin-Manuel Miranda-fronted “Hamilton” will all be screened for free as part of <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/things-to-do/2026/05/06/millennium-parks-summer-film-movie-series-full-schedule-lineup" ><u>Millennium Park’s Summer Film series.</u> </a></li><li>The park is also presenting a summer workout series every Saturday starting May 16 through Sept. 5. The 45-minute classes include pilates at 8 a.m.; yoga at 9 a.m.; cardio kickboxing at 10 a.m.; and Zumba at 11 a.m. at Wrigley Square and the Great Lawn.</li><li>The Grant Park Music Festival will perform Wednesdays, Fridays and Saturdays this summer in the Pritzker Pavilion. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/01/06/grant-park-festival-chicago-classical-millennium-park-music-free-ben-folds" target="_blank" ><u>Here’s what’s planned,</u></a> including a performance from Ben Folds of Ben Folds Five.</li></ul></div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Ambar Colón

entries.title: Millennium Park Summer Film series full 2026 schedule
entries.summary: <p>Millennium Park’s Summer Film series returns in full force in 2026 with a lineup of free movies, including a double feature on July 14 of films (“When Harry Met Sally” and “This is Spinal Tap”) directed by the late Rob Reiner.</p><p>Otherwise, the park will screen one movie every Tuesday (with one exception, on July 21) starting June 30 through August 18 on a 40-foot LED screen.</p><p>Got a little one who is a Disney fanatic? “Ratatouille” will screen on July 7. The Ryan Coogler-directed flick “Sinners,” which earned actor Michael B. Jordan his first Oscar, will close the outdoor film series on Aug 18.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a screen.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Walter S. Mitchell III/City of Chicago</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Here’s the full 2026 film schedule at Millennium Park:</b></h3><p><b>June 30</b>: “Independence Day”<br><b>July 7</b>: “Ratatouille”<br><b>July 14</b>: Rob Reiner double feature of “When Harry Met Sally” and “This is Spinal Tap”<br><b>July 28</b>: “Hamilton”<br><b>Aug. 4</b>: “The Lord of the Rings: The Fellowship of the Ring”<br><b>Aug. 11</b>: “The Devil Wears Prada”<br><b>Aug. 18</b>: “Sinners”</p><p>All screenings will be at the Jay Pritzker Pavilion in Millennium Park starting at 6:30 p.m. All are free to attend.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-000000" name="module-000000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3><b>More summer fun at Millennium Park:</b></h3><ul class="rte2-style-ul" id="rte-7108ea52-495a-11f1-9276-01ba0ff90a64"><li>The city is bringing lots of free music to the park this summer, from DJ sets to full-blown fests. Check out the full lineup <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/things-to-do/2026/05/06/millennium-park-summer-music-full-lineup-schedule" ><u>here</u></a>. </li><li>The park is also presenting a summer workout series every Saturday starting May 16 through Sept. 5. The 45-minute classes include pilates at 8 a.m.; yoga at 9 a.m.; cardio kickboxing at 10 a.m.; and Zumba at 11 a.m. at Wrigley Square and the Great Lawn.</li><li>The Grant Park Music Festival will perform Wednesdays, Fridays and Saturdays this summer in the Pritzker Pavilion. <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/01/06/grant-park-festival-chicago-classical-millennium-park-music-free-ben-folds" target="_blank" ><u>Here’s what’s planned,</u></a> including a performance from Ben Folds of Ben Folds Five.</li></ul></div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Sofie Hernandez-Simeonidis | WBEZ

entries.title: Some iPhone owners could get up to $95 payment after Apple agrees to settle case for $250 million
entries.summary: <p>Owners of some iPhones are in line to get cash payments of up to $95 from Apple after the company on Tuesday reached a $250 million settlement in a class-action lawsuit for false advertising of its artificial intelligence capabilities.</p><p>Apple trumpeted new AI features for its virtual assistant Siri when it rolled out the iPhone 16 in 2024, part of new software updates that the company billed as “Apple Intelligence.”</p><p>The company has been scrambling to keep up with tech rivals amid the AI boom but still hasn't delivered on the Siri revamp two years later.</p><p>The lawsuit, filed on behalf of consumers in the San Francisco federal court for the Northern District of California, alleged that Apple deceived consumers with a marketing campaign that promoted features that did not yet exist and misled them into buying the devices.</p><p>Lawyers for the iPhone buyers asked a court for preliminary approval of the proposed $250 million settlement, according to a court filling. If approved by a judge, it would be one of the biggest ever for Apple.</p><p>The settlement covers about 37 million devices bought in the U.S. between June 10, 2024 and March 29, 2025, including all iPhone 16 models and the iPhone 15 Pro and iPhone 15 Pro Max.</p><p>Owners are eligible for a payment of at least $25 for each device, and that amount could go up to $95 depending on how many other claims are filed “and other factors,” the filing said.</p><p>Customers will be notified by email or mail that they can file a claim on a settlement website, it said.</p><p>“Apple has reached a settlement to resolve claims related to the availability of two additional features,” the company said in a statement. "We resolved this matter to stay focused on doing what we do best, delivering the most innovative products and services to our users.”</p><p>Apple, based in Cupertino, California, was caught off-guard by the intense consumer interest in the Siri AI features. Buyers were angered after finding out that the new features would be released later than expected, the filing said.</p><p>They “would not have purchased the Eligible Devices or would have paid significantly less, had they known Enhanced Siri features were not available,” the filling said.</p><p>Apple's AI features remain in development even as rivals Google and Samsung have been rolling out more of the technology on their own devices. The company is expected to unveil its Siri upgrade this year, most likely at its annual developer conference next month.</p><p>Apple said in its statement that it has “introduced dozens of features” since it launched Apple Intelligence, such as Visual Intelligence and Live Translations.</p>
entries.author: Kelvin Chan | AP

entries.title: La Villita vive un Cinco de Mayo 'realmente lento' tras cancelación de desfile por segundo año consecutivo
entries.summary: <p>El desfile del Cinco de Mayo en Chicago solía atraer a grandes multitudes a La Villita, pero la celebración de este año fue aún más apagada que la del año pasado, según los dueños de negocios.</p><p>La reunión anual en el corazón de la comunidad mexicana de la ciudad fue cancelada por segundo año consecutivo debido a preocupaciones sobre la aplicación de la ley de inmigración, dijeron los organizadores el mes pasado.</p><p>Los propietarios comentaron que el desfile siempre ha sido vital para sus ventas, pero a lo largo de la calle 26 apenas había tráfico peatonal el martes por la tarde, tras ver menos clientes de lo habitual en los días previos a la festividad.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-210000" name="module-210000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>Para Francisca Alfaro Rodríguez, propietaria de Fran Arte y Estilo de México, el desfile solía atraer clientes a su tienda en busca de ropa tradicional mexicana. En cambio, comentó que apenas vendió algo durante el fin de semana festivo.</p><p>“Solía haber tanta alegría aquí”, dijo Alfaro Rodríguez. “Había orgullo en vestir ropa mexicana”.</p><p>El desfile, que conmemora la inesperada victoria de México sobre el ejército francés de Napoleón III en la Batalla de Puebla de 1862, históricamente ha atraído a cientos al vecindario. Pero los organizadores <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/04/21/cancelan-desfile-del-cinco-de-mayo-en-la-villita-por-segundo-ano-no-hay-nada-que-celebrar" target="_blank" >dijeron al Sun-Times</a> que la comunidad mexicana de Chicago todavía está en alerta tras el Operativo Midway Blitz, la campaña de deportación de la administración de Trump en el área de Chicago el otoño pasado.</p><p>Alfaro Rodríguez indicó que las ventas han disminuido casi un 90% desde el año pasado y esperaba que el desfile pudiera ayudar a compensar esto. En cambio, las calles estaban mayormente vacías el martes.</p><p>“Dicen que las redadas han disminuido, pero la gente aún no sale”, comentó.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div un día ocupado para ellos, pero la tienda estaba vacía el martes por la tarde.</p><p>“La calle solía estar llena de gente, pero este año fue realmente lento”, dijo.</p><p>Ortiz expresó que entiende por qué la gente podría tener miedo de salir, recordando cómo vio a personas corriendo en busca de seguridad durante las redadas de inmigración el otoño pasado. Agregó que el vecindario se convirtió en un blanco fácil durante la campaña de deportación porque los residentes celebran abiertamente su herencia mexicana.</p><p>La disminución del tráfico peatonal ha afectado a los negocios de todo el vecindario. Ortiz indicó que antes de las redadas, su tienda tenía cuatro empleados trabajando durante el horario comercial, pero ahora sólo tiene dos.</p><p>“Lo que pasó el año pasado fue triste, pero espero que la gente regrese”, agregó Ortiz.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Strike ends after 3 days as Brookfield Zoo, union workers agree on 4-year contract
entries.summary: <p>A union strike at Brookfield Zoo has ended on its third day after zoo officials and union workers signed a four-year collective bargaining agreement.</p><p>The agreement covers employees in animal care, facilities, custodial and security roles and "reflects a shared commitment to moving forward together in support of the Zoo’s mission," the zoo said in a statement. </p><p>Employees are expected to return to work once the agreement is finalized.</p><p>"Today is an important and positive moment for Brookfield Zoo Chicago, and we are excited to welcome our team members back as we move forward together in our shared dedication to our people, the animals in our care, and the communities we serve,” said Dr. Mike Adkesson, President and CEO of Brookfield Zoo Chicago in the statement. “This Zoo has always been powered by people who care deeply about one another and our mission, and this agreement reflects a commitment to continuing to provide exceptional care and educational experiences for our guests while supporting the long-term health and sustainability of the Zoo.”</p><p>The proposal includes a 20% wage increase over the course of the 4-year contract and the zoo will continue providing enhanced healthcare contributions and enhancements to staffing for the Zoo’s animal care and facilities teams, in addition to three positions already posted for hiring.</p><p>“We appreciate the dedication of our staff, the professionalism of the bargaining teams, and the patience and support of our guests and community during this time,” Adkesson said. “We are focused on continuing the important work of our mission and advancing our animal care, conservation, and education priorities.”</p><p>Dozens of Brookfield Zoo Chicago <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2026/05/04/some-brookfield-zoo-staffers-strike-after-negotiations-fail" target="_blank" >union workers, pickets in hand, made their voices heard</a> outside the main entrance on Monday — the first day of an authorized strike after failed contract negotiations.</p><p>Brookfield Zoo workers <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/04/29/brookfield-zoo-workers-vote-to-strike-after-failed-union-contract-negotiations" target="_blank" >authorized the strike</a> amid failed union contract negotiations in late April. </p>
entries.author: Mohammad Samra

entries.title: 6 hurt in Des Plaines blaze
entries.summary: <p>Six people suffered injuries in an apartment fire in Des Plaines early Wednesday morning, according to multiple media reports.</p><p>Des Plaines fire officials have yet to release any details about the blaze at the three-story apartment complex on the 1200 block of Harding Avenue.</p><p>The extent of injuries was unknown, but witnesses reported hearing an explosion before the fire erupted, according to <a class="Link" href="https://wgntv.com/news/chicago-news/5-injured-in-early-morning-apartment-fire-in-des-plaines/?fbclid=IwY2xjawRoGvBleHRuA2FlbQIxMABicmlkETFBemEwa3VWZEpTTDdBa1NZc3J0YwZhcHBfaWQQMjIyMDM5MTc4ODIwMDg5MgABHmGK1Vhr_D585diEa0MC-3vJYGin80Jp71-5AnSze6SpeyhUtfW7cREMTq4w_aem_FghaB2OvTHPiw2wAAix_nA" target="_blank" >a WGN news report</a>.</p><p>For more on this story, please visit <a class="Link" href="https://www.dailyherald.com/20260506/news/report-six-people-hurt-in-des-plaines-apartment-fire/" target="_blank" >DailyHerald.com</a>.</p>

target="_blank" >Illinois State Police launch investigation into deadly ICE shooting</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-d/" target="_blank" >Sophie Sherry</a></h5><p><b>Probe of shooting</b>: Illinois State Police have opened an investigation into the fatal shooting of Silverio Villegas González, nearly eight months after he was killed by a federal immigration agent during a traffic stop in Franklin Park about the time Operation Midway Blitz started.</p><p><b>Local police request</b>: Franklin Park police asked ISP to investigate Thursday, when a state commission released a damning report detailing the actions of federal agents during President Donald Trump’s aggressive deportation campaign in the Chicago area last year. The report urged Illinois law enforcement agencies to investigate and prosecute alleged crimes stemming from Midway Blitz.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: The ISP probe is the most high-profile independent inquiry announced regarding the immigration enforcement operation, during which federal agents also shot Marimar Martinez in Brighton Park and routinely used pummeling force and chemical irritants. The investigation comes amid a push to install a special prosecutor in the Cook County state’s attorney’s office to target federal agents who carried out Midway Blitz.</p><p><a class="Link" target="_blank" >Cook County tax official cleared of drunken driving charge</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-u/" target="_blank" >Sophie Sherry</a></h5><p><b>Official acquittal</b>: Cook County Board of Review Commissioner Samantha Steele was acquitted of drunken driving Tuesday after a two-day trial that focused on a November 2024 crash and the police's response.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Steele was arrested after her car hit a parked vehicle. Officers found an open bottle of wine in her car, but she never submitted to field sobriety or lab tests. In bodycam footage, Steele repeatedly told officers she was an elected official and refused to cooperate. She also made disrespectful comments to one officer about his genitals, records show.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-o/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>SMALL BUSINESS 💰</h4><div class="Enhancement" target="_blank" >Little Village sees muted Cinco de Mayo after parade is canceled for second straight year</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-n/" target="_blank" >Cindy Hernandez</a></h5><p><b>Cinco de Mayo</b>: Chicago’s Cinco de Mayo parade once drew large crowds to Little Village, but this year’s celebration Tuesday was even more muted than last year, according to business owners. The annual holiday gathering in the heart of the city’s Mexican community was canceled for the second year in a row due to immigration enforcement concerns, organizers said last month.</p><p><b>Business POV</b>: Small business owners say the parade has always been vital to sales. Along 26th Street, there was hardly any foot traffic early Tuesday afternoon, after fewer customers than usual were seen in the days leading up to the holiday.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-p/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>MORE NEWS YOU NEED 🗞️</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-370000" name="image-370000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/dd72a78/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+65/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2F1c%2Fe807d92a4c1b8c13a203125999c6%2Fmarket-260506-07.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/5d0ec6c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+65/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2F1c%2Fe807d92a4c1b8c13a203125999c6%2Fmarket-260506-07.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2c616f3/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6122x3436+0+65/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7f%2F1c%2Fe807d92a4c1b8c13a203125999c6%2Fmarket-260506-07.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Exterior of the Market Hall at 11159 S. href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-s/" target="_blank" >Juanpablo Ramirez-Franco</a></h5><p><b>Sale returns</b>: This weekend marks the 30th annual Kilbourn Park plant sale, a massively popular event that draws hundreds who up around the park waiting to snatch up plants at $4 apiece.</p><p><b>Native plant twist</b>: For decades, Chicago gardeners flocked to the Kilbourn Park sale to pick up tomatoes, cucumbers and annuals. This year, the park responded to a relatively new demand: Nearly 1 in 5 plants for sale are native species that have adapted to local climate and wildlife and are generally low maintenance.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: That demand has been driven in part by concerns about dramatic declines in insect species. Caterpillars of the monarch butterfly, for example, depend on native milkweed as a food source. Even backyard gardeners face looming concerns about climate change-powered extreme heat, drought and flooding.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-g/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>ON WBEZ 91.5 FM 📻 </h4><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-yh/" target="_blank" ><b>In the Loop with Sasha-Ann Simons, 9 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-471ea995-494c-11f1-83d2-c3cfd6f2ec22"><li><b>Beach parking</b>: New pay-to-park barriers are popping up at some Chicago beaches. The Park District says they will generate more than $9 million in revenue. Community members are concerned about beach access. Sherry Williams of the Burnham Park Advisory Council, Brian Gladstein of Friends of the Parks and Ald. Lamont Robinson dig into both sides.<br></li><li><b>Pharmacy deserts</b>: Ald. Robinson and Mari Gallagher of Mari Gallagher Research and Consulting Group discuss the impact when areas in the city are devoid of pharmacies.</li></ul><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-yk/" target="_blank" ><b>Say More with Mary Dixon and Patrick Smith, 10 a.m.</b></a></p><ul class="rte2-style-ul" style="line-height: inherit; margin-top: 0px; margin-bottom: 20px; margin-left: 24px; padding: 0px; list-style-type: disc;" id="rte-471ea998-494c-11f1-83d2-c3cfd6f2ec22"><li><b>Bad bridges</b>: A <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-yu/" target="_blank" >recent report from the Sun-Times</a> shows one in six Chicago bridges are in "poor" condition. target="_blank" >New club Decibel opens in former Berlin space on Belmont</a></h3><h3></h3><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-jk/" target="_blank" >Brittany Sowacke</a></h5><p>Steps from the Red Line, Decibel quickly filled up Saturday night as the queue stretched down the block for the new nightclub’s opening weekend.</p><p>Those in line said they'd hoped to rekindle the flame that went out Nov. 19, 2023 — the day the historic Berlin nightclub closed after more than 40 years of serving the North Side LGBTQ community. Many wondered: Could new life settle in at 954 W. Belmont Ave.?</p><p>That new life is a dual-bar concept, The Belmont/Decibel, announced last year by HV Entertainment. The Belmont is a relaxed cocktail bar early in the evening, but after 10 p.m., a curtain is pulled back to reveal the revamped dance floor and transform into Decibel.</p><p>"It’s kinda surreal to be back here," said Andrew Rogers, 34. "I never thought I’d return to the space. Thankfully, it still has the same energy."</p><p>His friend, Riley Rogers, 37, agreed: "Having the sense of community is very important to me. Berlin brought so many queer and alt people together."</p><p>Later, music boomed from a DJ's set and body heat from the dancefloor fogged up the venue's windows.</p><p>"It’s not about the name — it’s about the vibes," bartender Derek Stuch said.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-ju/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>YOUR DAILY QUESTION ☕️</h4><p><b>After companies charged consumers higher prices on items due to the Trump administration's tariffs, what do you think of </b><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wdtithy-l-tr/" target="_blank" ><b>companies getting refunds from the government</b></a><b>?</b></p><p><a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" ><i>Respond to this newsletter</i></a><i> (please include your first and last name). We may run your answer in a future newsletter or story.</i></p><hr><p>Thanks for reading the Sun-Times Morning Edition!<br>Got a story you think we missed? <a class="Link" href="mailto:newsletters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >Email us here.</a></p><hr><p><b>Written and curated by</b>: Matt Moore<br><b>Editor</b>: Eydie Cubarrubia</p><hr><p><i>The Chicago Sun-Times is a nonprofit supported by readers like you. entries.title: Giant slide at new Griffin Museum of Science and Industry exhibit set to be a scene stealer
entries.summary: <p>Some might get a queasy feeling looking over the railing at the top of the 28-foot-high slide that anchors a new exhibit at the Griffin Museum of Science and Industry</p><p>Others will begin thinking "Wheeeeee!" before even entering the mouth of the snaking steel tube.</p><p>It takes about ten seconds to reach the bottom, enough time to sing "Happy Birthday" on the way down.</p><p>"It's huge, huge, huge — what is there to say? I mean, it's incredible," said Patricia Ward, the museum's head scientist. "We've been testing it, and it really is kind of thrilling."</p><p>The slide is part of 'Powering the Future,' a new permanent exhibit opening May 8 at the South Side museum. The exhibit is included in the price of general admission.</p><p>The exhibit explores the different forms of energy that power our lives. Kids move through the slide as if they are electrons moving through the power grid, Ward said.</p><p>There are circular windows on the ceiling of the tube and snaking lights on its exterior that light up as kids descend.</p><p>Children must be at least 44 inches tall and at least six years old to go down the slide, which must be taken feet first.</p><p>"It provides a visceral sense that energy is in everything and is everywhere, including you," said Ward, who says she has gone down the slide herself.</p><p>A handle bar above the mouth of the slide gives kids something to hold on to as they lower themselves into the tube, or it can be used to fling oneself into the ride.</p><p>"I would make sure that you grab the handles and you pull yourself and you go really fast," said Hannah Holt, 13, whose family bid at a charity auction to be the first to go down the slide before it opens to the public.</p><p>A spiral staircase that follows the path of the slide is available for anyone who doesn't want to cruise down on their backside.</p><p>"I can say that I don't really like heights, but it was very fun," Ward said.</p><p>She concedes many kids will rush past other cool stuff in the exhibit — like an electric car from 1923 and a nuclear fuel rod assembly — to get in line for the slide.</p><p>"But that's OK. That's OK," she said with a smile during a recent tour.</p><p>The slide is definitely worth a try but don't miss the opportunity to touch the Ice Wall — yes, a wall of ice — as an infrared camera captures your thermal footprint and projects the real-time image on a nearby monitor. It's cool. </p>
entries.author: Mitch Dudek

entries.title: Chicago Park District installs automated parking gates at 10 beaches
entries.summary: <p>Beachgoers may notice changes at several lakefront parking lots citywide this summer as the city rolls out a new “modernized” payment management system. </p><p>The Chicago Park District installed automated parking gates at 10 lots on the city’s lakefront and is using license plate readers to track when drivers enter and exit.</p><p>But paying at the lots isn’t new. The park district says the recent changes “simply modernize how payment is managed and enforced.” The park district also said the new gates will help with park security.</p><p>The lots, managed by SP+, have been paid lots since 2009.</p><p>Under the system, visitors will be allowed a 15-minute grace period free of charge that can be used for pickup, drop-off or unloading supplies. After that, a parking fee will be applied. Parking rates have not increased as a result of the upgrades, according to the park district.</p><p>Parking rates vary slightly by location, with fees around $4.07 for up to one hour and a maximum of $24 for vehicles parked longer than nine hours.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" entries.author: Cindy Hernandez

entries.title: Final season of FX's 'The Bear' to premiere June 25
entries.summary: <p>"The Bear" is coming to an end this summer — and now fans can save the date.</p><p>The final season of the Chicago-based hit TV show is set to air beginning June 25, according to a news release from FX. It will air on FX and be available to stream on Hulu and Disney+.</p><p>SPOILER ALERT: The final season will pick up after Season 4's cliffhanger ending, in which troubled-but-brilliant chef Carmy Berzatto (Jeremy Allen White) walks away from the food business and leaves the restaurant to Sydney (Ayo Edebiri), Richie (Ebon Moss-Bachrach) and Natalie “Sugar” (Abby Elliott).</p><p>The news of the final season's premiere comes a day after FX unexpectedly <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/arts-and-culture/2026/05/05/the-bear-new-episode-fifth-season-road-trip-gary-indiana" target="_blank" >dropped a flashback episode </a>following Richie and Carmy's brother Mikey (Jon Bernthal) on a road trip to Gary, Indiana. The first season of the show picked up shortly after Mikey's suicide prompted Carmy to leave his fine dining career in New York and help save the family sandwich shop.</p><p>Chicagoans saw themselves in the hit show, which is loosely based on the River North restaurant Mr. Beef and has filmed in Chicago for years. The show has brought new fans to some of Chicago's most respected restaurants that have been featured on the show, including Filipino bakery Kasama in West Town and Grant Achatz’s Alinea. Chicagoans even competed to see who most resembled the show's lead, Jeremy Allen White, in a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/entertainment-and-culture/2024/11/19/jeremy-allen-white-look-alike-winner-ben-shabad-embracing-odd-but-exciting-contest-experience-toddler-lookalike-also-revealed" >look-alike contest</a> in 2024.</p><p>That the upcoming season would be the series' last and has been <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/movies-and-tv/2026/03/06/fx-the-bear-to-end-after-fifth-season-report-says" target="_blank" >expected for months</a>, though a premiere date was never confirmed. Recurring guest star Jamie Lee Curtis told <a class="Link" href="https://www.etonline.com/media/videos/jamie-lee-curtis-on-if-the-bear-is-really-ending-and-taking-on-murder-she-wrote-240415" target="_blank" ><u>Entertainment Tonight</u></a> in March, “It is the end of the show. Everybody knows it’s the end of the show. I’m not breaking any news to anybody.”</p><p>The final season will begin with two episodes dropped on June 25, with one episode following weekly, according to FX. </p><p><i>Contributing: Courtney Kueppers</i></p>
entries.author: Mary Norkol

entries.title: Policía del Estado de Illinois inicia investigación sobre tiroteo mortal de Silverio Villegas González por ICE
entries.summary: <p>La Policía del Estado de Illinois (ISP) ha comenzado una investigación sobre el tiroteo fatal de Silverio Villegas González, casi ocho meses después de que fuera asesinado por un agente federal de inmigración durante una parada de tráfico en el suburbio de Franklin Park, al inicio del Operativo Midway Blitz.</p><p>La Policía de Franklin Park solicitó el jueves a la ISP que investigara cuando una comisión estatal publicó un informe contundente que detalla las acciones de los agentes federales durante la agresiva campaña de deportación del presidente Donald Trump en el área de Chicago el año pasado.</p><p>El informe instó a las agencias de aplicación de la ley de Illinois a investigar y procesar los presuntos delitos derivados del Operativo Midway Blitz.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-410000" name="module-410000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>La investigación de la ISP es la indagación independiente de mayor perfil que se ha anunciado en respuesta al operativo de control de inmigración que duró varios meses, durante la cual los agentes federales también le dispararon a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/04/17/marimar-martinez-a-quien-le-disparo-5-veces-un-agente-federal-testificara-en-audiencia-en-washington" target="_blank" >Marimar Martínez</a> en el vecindario de Brighton Park y utilizaron de manera rutinaria fuerza excesiva y productos químicos irritantes.</p><p>La investigación se lanzó en medio de un impulso legal para <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/03/13/coalicion-dice-que-fiscal-del-condado-de-cook-eileen-oneill-burke-ignoro-la-violencia-de-ice" target="_blank" >designar un fiscal especial</a> en la Fiscalía del Estado del Condado de Cook para investigar a los agentes federales que llevaron a cabo la campaña.</p><p>Una coalición de funcionarios electos, clérigos, periodistas y abogados ha argumentado que la Fiscal del Estado Eileen O’Neill Burke “tiene una alianza con las fuerzas del orden federal que le impide tomar medidas contra los agentes federales que supuestamente han cometido delitos durante el Operativo Midway Blitz”.</p><p>El martes, la oficina de O’Neill Burke dijo que los fiscales fueron contactados por la ISP y desempeñarán un “rol de apoyo” en la investigación, siguiendo las directrices de la oficina para manejar casos que involucren a agentes federales.</p><p>“No podemos comentar más sobre una investigación de aplicación de la ley pendiente”, aseguró la oficina de O’Neill Burke en un comunicado.</p><p>Villegas González, un papá y cocinero originario de Michoacán, México, fue <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/09/12/oficial-de-ice-dispara-fatalmente-a-un-hombre-durante-una-detencion-de-trafico-en-un-suburbio-de-chicago" target="_blank" >asesinado</a> “a quemarropa” el 12 de septiembre de 2025, según un informe de la autopsia. Supuestamente estaba tratando de huir de los agentes del Servicio de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) que intentaron detener su automóvil.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-4d4829" name="image-4d4829"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/849854c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1200x673+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F7a%2F2c345d004e329e0fb8a7973b969d%2Fsilverio-villegas-y-sus-hijos-cortesia-gofundme-familia-villegas-img-8294.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/946ebcf/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1200x673+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F7a%2F2c345d004e329e0fb8a7973b969d%2Fsilverio-villegas-y-sus-hijos-cortesia-gofundme-familia-villegas-img-8294.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/28bd35e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1200x673+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F7a%2F2c345d004e329e0fb8a7973b969d%2Fsilverio-villegas-y-sus-hijos-cortesia-gofundme-familia-villegas-img-8294.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Silverio Villegas González" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/28bd35e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1200x673+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F7a%2F2c345d004e329e0fb8a7973b969d%2Fsilverio-villegas-y-sus-hijos-cortesia-gofundme-familia-villegas-img-8294.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/093bfcb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1200x673+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F7a%2F2c345d004e329e0fb8a7973b969d%2Fsilverio-villegas-y-sus-hijos-cortesia-gofundme-familia-villegas-img-8294.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/28bd35e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1200x673+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Feb%2F7a%2F2c345d004e329e0fb8a7973b969d%2Fsilverio-villegas-y-sus-hijos-cortesia-gofundme-familia-villegas-img-8294.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Silverio Villegas González y sus hijos.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>GoFundMe</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Una bala impactó el cuello de Villegas González, según reveló el informe. Fue trasladado de urgencia al Centro Médico de la Universidad Loyola en Maywood, donde fue declarado muerto menos de una hora después.</p><p>El Buró Federal de Investigaciones (FBI) inicialmente respondió a la escena del tiroteo y, según la ISP, fue la “agencia principal de investigación”. La ISP no quiso comentar sobre los detalles de su nueva investigación. La Policía de Franklin Park no respondió de inmediato a las solicitudes de comentarios.</p><p>El tiroteo ocurrió apenas unos días después de que la administración de Trump iniciara el Operativo Midway Blitz.</p><p>Los funcionarios del Departamento de Seguridad Nacional (DHS) afirmaron inicialmente que un agente de ICE había sido “arrastrado a una distancia significativa” por el automóvil de Villegas González y que había sufrido “lesiones graves”. Pero en las <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2025/09/23/video-muestra-que-agente-de-ice-dijo-que-sus-heridas-no-eran-graves-en-tiroteo-de-silverio-villegas-gonzalez" target="_blank" >grabaciones de la cámara corporal</a> de la escena, el agente describió sus lesiones como “nada grave”.</p><p>Han aumentado las solicitudes para que las autoridades de Illinois y los fiscales responsabilicen a los agentes federales por las tácticas agresivas empleadas durante el Operativo Midway Blitz. Las agencias locales en Minnesota han adoptado un enfoque mucho más agresivo, lanzando rápidamente investigaciones sobre los tiroteos fatales de Renee Good y de Alex Pretti.</p><p>La oficina de la Fiscal del Estado del Condado de Cook ha sostenido que está limitada en su capacidad para llevar a cabo investigaciones. Según <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/02/20/fiscal-principal-del-condado-de-cook-presenta-plan-para-acusar-a-agentes-de-ice-de-delitos" target="_blank" >directrices publicadas por la oficina</a> en febrero, las agencias de aplicación de la ley deben liderar las investigaciones criminales y luego presentar evidencia a los fiscales para su revisión y posible acusación.</p><p>Mientras tanto, la Comisión de Responsabilidad de Illinois, formada por el gobernador JB Pritzker, lleva meses realizando su propia investigación, recopilando testimonios y revisando grabaciones de video y documentos legales. El <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz/2026/05/04/comision-que-investiga-el-operativo-midway-blitz-sienta-las-bases-para-una-verdadera-rendicion-de-cuentas" target="_blank" >informe final</a> de la comisión, publicado el jueves, reveló que los agentes federales actuaron ilegalmente y con impunidad durante sus esfuerzos de aplicación de la ley de inmigración.</p><p>El informe incluyó una carta refiriendo cargos a O’Neill Burke, al Departamento de Policía de Chicago, a la Fiscal del Estado del Condado de Kane, y a los departamentos de policía de Elgin, Franklin Park y Evanston.</p><p>O’Neill Burke respondió que no podía presentar cargos sin una remisión de una agencia de aplicación de la ley.</p><p><i>Contribuyó: Jon Seidel </i></p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por </i><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Sophie Sherry

entries.title: Kentucky Derby winner Golden Tempo will skip Preakness
entries.summary: <p>Kentucky Derby winner Golden Tempo will not run in the Preakness Stakes next weekend, trainer Cherie DeVaux announced Wednesday.</p><p>DeVaux and owners decided to skip the Preakness and set their sights on the Belmont Stakes on June 6 at Saratoga Race Course in upstate New York. DeVaux, who became the first woman to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports/2026/05/02/golden-tempo-takes-kentucky-derby-as-cherie-devaux-becomes-first-woman-to-train-its-winner" >train a Derby winner,</a> is from Saratoga Springs, which is hosting the Belmont for a third and final time this year.</p><p>"We are incredibly appreciative of the excitement and support surrounding the possibility of a Triple Crown run," DeVaux said in a statement. "Golden gave us the race of a lifetime in the Kentucky Derby, and we believe the best decision for him moving forward is to give him a little more time following such a tremendous effort. His health, happiness and long-term future will always remain our top priority."</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/sports/2026/05/02/golden-tempo-takes-kentucky-derby-as-cherie-devaux-becomes-first-woman-to-train-its-winner" >Golden Tempo takes Kentucky Derby as Cherie DeVaux becomes first woman to train winner</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Golden Tempo is the third Derby winner in the past five years not to be entered in the Preakness. For various reasons, it is the sixth time in eight years the Preakness will happen with no chance of a Triple Crown on the line. American Pharoah in 2015 and Justify in 2018 are the only horses to sweep all three races over the past four decades.</p><p>The two-week turnaround from the Derby to the Preakness, which used to be commonplace, is considered a nonstarter for many trainers and owners given that most elite thoroughbreds now typically go a month or more between races. It has caused endless debate in horse racing circles about the spacing of the Triple Crown in modern times.</p><p>Maryland racing officials are considering moving the Preakness back from the third Saturday in May to the fourth to increase the chances of not just the winner but other horses from the Derby being considered for the second leg of the Triple Crown. None of the 18 who ran this year at Churchill Downs are heading to the Preakness, with Golden Tempo the only one considered.</p><p>The Preakness is taking place at Laurel Park between Baltimore and Washington, D.C., this spring while its longtime home, Pimlico Race Course, is rebuilt as part of a massive construction project that included demolishing the debilitating old structure. Pimlico is set to become the site for year-round racing in Maryland beginning next year when the state takes control from 1/ST Racing, with Laurel becoming a training venue.</p><p>Golden Tempo won the Kentucky Derby as a 23-1 long shot in spectacular fashion, making a charge from the back of the pack down the stretch to the finish line a neck ahead of morning line favorite Renegade. DeVaux and co-owner Daisy Phipps Pulito said they would see how the colt came out of the race before making any decisions.</p><p>They followed the lead of trainer Bill Mott and Godolphin Racing, which last year chose to bypass the Preakness with Derby champion Sovereignty to give him extra rest for the Belmont. Sovereignty rewarded them by winning the Belmont and the Travers Stakes and is back racing as a 4-year-old.<br></p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Illinois attorney general pushes for rate cut as Peoples Gas asks for $202M hike
entries.summary: <p>The Illinois Attorney General’s office and consumer advocates say Peoples Gas’ proposal to raise rates by $202 million would ultimately cost homeowners while lining the pockets of the utility’s shareholders, and proposed a rate decrease instead.</p><p>It will be up to the Illinois Commerce Commission to rule later this year on the <a class="Link" href="https://www.icc.illinois.gov/downloads/public/filing/4/395055.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>gas company’s request</u></a> for a $202 million rate hike, which would bump gas bills by an average of around $130 per year.</p><p>But the Illinois Attorney General’s office suggested a $4.1 million decrease instead, listing at least $97.2 million in “unsupported and unexplained” cost increases for the company’s pipe retirement program, among other “unsubstantiated” charges.</p><p>"[Peoples Gas estimated] costs are nearly double historical costs on a dollar per mile basis and the increase is not supported by the evidence provided to date," the office wrote in a filing. "[The requests] require further review and appear to contain data that is contradictory, incomplete, and otherwise unreliable, indicating that included cost estimates are also likely to be unreliable."</p><p>Peoples Gas said the rate hike was needed because of inflation-boosting costs and to continue work on its ongoing pipe retirement program to ensure "safety and reliability in the system."</p><p>"A significant reduction in funding level would affect how quickly the work can be done, or whether the work can be done at all," a company spokesperson said in a statement.</p><p>The proposed hike was requested just two years after the Illinois Commerce Commission granted the utility company a record <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/consumer-affairs/2023/11/16/23963198/peoples-gas-rate-hike-pipeline-replacement-program-illinois-commerce-commission" target="_blank" ><u>$306 million rate hike</u></a>; it had originally sought a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/business/2023/1/6/23542395/peoples-gas-rate-hike-request-illinois-commerce-commission-energy" target="_blank" ><u>$402 million increase</u></a>.</p><p>Raoul’s office said the utility potentially “double counted” in cost estimates for the program, including adding costs for design work that’s years old, multiplying the labor force on projects of “similar complexity” — sometimes by a factor of 10 — and the request of a $71 million contingency fund it says remains without clear goals while escaping some of the office’s previous regulations.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/03/peoples-gas-hike-city-hall" >Peoples Gas holds firm on proposed $202M price hike at City Hall hearing</a> </li> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/consumer-affairs/2025/02/20/peoples-gas-pipeline-program-icc-decision" >State regulators clamp down on Peoples Gas pipeline replacement program</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The testimony comes a week after Peoples Gas and North Shore Gas reached a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/04/30/peoples-gas-north-shore-gas-125-million-settlement-raoul-attorney-general" target="_blank" ><u>$125 million settlement</u></a> with Raoul’s office because the state alleged some money collected between 2017 and 2023 was incurred on “ineligible” expenses. The agreement has the company cutting $130 million in capital costs and further regulates contract bids, cost changes and other reporting requirements for its ongoing <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/consumer-affairs/2025/02/20/peoples-gas-pipeline-program-icc-decision" target="_blank" ><u>pipe retirement program</u></a>.</p><p>It began in February 2025 after state regulators axed its predecessor, the Accelerated Main Replacement Program, a year earlier. Costs for that project had ballooned to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2021/2/16/22286529/peoples-gas-pipe-replacement-consumer-bills-chicago-illinois-pirg-lightfoot-pritzker" target="_blank" ><u>almost six times the original estimate</u></a> while it ran behind schedule. The attorney general’s office's testimony warns of a potentially similar fate for the current program. </p><p>Peoples Gas expects to replace 21 of the more than 1,000 miles of pipeline left this year and 53 miles next year, per its own estimates. Raoul’s office estimates at this rate it will be done in 2039, unless the utility can bump the average to 135 miles per year. On a quarterly investor call Tuesday, Scott Lauber, CEO of Peoples Gas' parent company, WEC Energy Group, said the utility <a class="Link" href="https://investor.wecenergygroup.com/investors/earnings/default.aspx" target="_blank" ><u>planned to file annual rate hikes</u></a> because of the ongoing work.</p><p>“It’s already so expensive to be a Peoples Gas customer,” said Citizens Utility Board counsel Eric DeBellis. “People are already hurting… Every increase in the cost of necessities is drastic.”</p><p>DeBellis is among those with CUB, Illinois PIRG and other consumer advocates who are making their own case for an at least 66% cut to the Peoples Gas proposal.</p><p>Estimates from the groups say the company’s request for a better payout for investors, suggesting a 10.1% return on equity, could be reduced to 9.45% — something also reflected in the attorney general’s office’s proposal. It would still be an increase from the current 9.38%, meaning a bigger possible payout still for shareholders, but it would cut the utility’s proposed hike by $42.8 million.</p><p> WEC Energy Group reported $1.6 billion in profits in 2025 after the company saw revenue grow eightfold between 2024 and 2025. Its stockholders profited to the tune of <a class="Link" href="https://s22.q4cdn.com/994559668/files/doc_earnings/2026/q1/generic/2026-Q1-WEC-Earnings-Package-Final.pdf" target="_blank" ><u>$804.4 million in the first quarter</u></a> of 2026.</p><p>Meanwhile <a class="Link" href="https://www.icc.illinois.gov/industry-reports/credit-collections-and-arrearages-reports/monthly-dashboard" target="_blank" ><u>140,000 Chicago households</u></a> were at least 30 days behind on their gas bill in March, totaling more than $100 million in debt, according to ICC data. In response to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2026/03/03/peoples-gas-hike-city-hall" >concerns about affordability earlier this year</a>, the company told a Chicago City Council committee it was working with the city to help residents find discounts.</p><p>The advocates’ analysis also says $14.4 million could be cut from profit incentive-based executive bonuses. ICC staff made a similar recommendation in their own filings, saying it "provides benefits to shareholders and not ratepayers."</p><p>“This company is rolling in profits, their parent company is rolling in profits,” said Jim Chilsen, CUB’s communications director. “And here we are again with them in Chicagoans' pockets.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div entries.author: Violet Miller

entries.title: Botched raid victim Anjanette Young slated for Chicago police oversight role
entries.summary: <p>Anjanette Young, a social worker who became a police reform advocate after an infamous<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/6/28/23777147/anjanette-young-wrong-raids-chicago-police-department-deborah-witzberg-inspector-general" > botched raid </a>on her home in 2019, is one step closer to becoming a bureaucrat with sway over police policy.</p><p>That’s after the Chicago City Council’s Police and Fire Committee lauded Young’s reform work and voted 14-2 Wednesday to confirm her as one of seven commissioners on the Community Commission for Public Safety and Accountability.</p><p>In 2019, Young stood naked and handcuffed for nearly 10 minutes before officers allowed her to get dressed as they carried out a search warrant on what turned out to be the wrong home.</p><p>“It was violating. It was life-altering, but it did not break me. It actually shaped my purpose,” Young told committee members. “It strengthened my resolve to help rebuild trust between community and law enforcement through meaningful, lasting change.”</p><p>Since 2019, Young has pushed for state- and city-level bans on no-knock warrants, so far to no avail. A state measure dubbed the Anjanette Young Act that would ban no-knock warrants and largely prohibit police officers from pointing guns at children sits in the House Rules Committee.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/police-reform/2024/02/16/anjanette-young-chicago-police-no-knock-warrants-botched-raid" >Anjanette Young continues fight to ban no-knock warrants 5 years after botched CPD raid</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>In Chicago, Police Supt. Larry Snelling has opposed a ban on no-knock warrants, and in 2024, a judge who oversees the federal consent decree bounding CPD declined to impose certain search warrant restrictions.</p><p>But outcry after the raid and Young’s efforts have led to a new CPD search warrant policy that stipulates police should only execute search warrants between 6 a.m. and 9 p.m., unless there’s an emergency. It requires officers to delay entry to give residents reasonable time to respond. It also acknowledges that search warrants can be “traumatic and intrusive” and requires officers to minimize trauma in various ways, such as “avoid[ing] intentionally pointing firearms at children.”</p><p>In her remarks, Young appeared to appeal to more conservative committee members who might feel her reform work makes her an enemy of the police.</p><p>“Over the past seven years, part of my healing journey has included collaborating with and learning from top officers within CPD,” Young said. “That work has reinforced my belief that accountability and collaboration are not opposite forces… My lived experience does not compromise my ability to be fair.”</p><p>Ald. Chris Taliaferro (29th), a former Chicago police sergeant and chair of the council’s police and fire panel, commended Young for her conciliatory tone.</p><p>“One of the things that I so respect about you is that you never took a moment to talk bad of our police department,” Taliaferro said. “After you've gone through the horrific situation that you did… you chose a path that is so much identifiable in your character, and that's to look at policies: How can we change policies to make sure that this does not happen again?”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/news/2021/11/10/22774338/anjanette-young-botched-chicago-police-raid-sgt-alex-wolinski-copa-civilian-office-accountability" >CPD sergeant should be fired over botched Anjanette Young raid, top cop says; other officers also face discipline</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>Alds. Silvana Tabares (23rd) and Anthony Napolitano (41st) voted against Young’s appointment.</p><p>Tabares, a staunch opponent of police oversight reform, took a sharp tone and raised her voice to admonish Young and appeared to partially blame violence against police on Young’s reform efforts.</p><p>“You're before the committee today seeking an appointment to a commission borne out of the same movement that also saw the skyrocketing of attacks on police since its creation,” Tabares said. “We need individuals who are avoiding dangerous, anti-police rhetoric that incites violence.”</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson, who campaigned on passing an ordinance in Young’s name to outright ban no-knock warrants, selected Young for the nomination. Her appointment still requires full council approval.</p><p>If approved, Young would be one of seven commissioners responsible for drafting and reviewing policies for the police department, as well as the policies of two other agencies involved in police oversight.</p><p>She would also have some sway over who leads CPD, with the ability to recommend a police superintendent’s hiring or firing.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0d0000" name="image-0d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/429d519/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3096x1738+0+163/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdb%2F3a%2F073b9ee6c4462d08770b2366ddef%2Fcpdraid-121720-15.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b46af34/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3096x1738+0+163/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdb%2F3a%2F073b9ee6c4462d08770b2366ddef%2Fcpdraid-121720-15.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Anjanette Young, who was a victim of a botched raid by the Chicago Police Department in 2019, tears up as she speaks to the press outside the Chicago Police Department headquarters Dec. 16, 2020. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>If she wins full council backing, Young would assume her role after the current commissioner’s term expires in June.</p><p>Angelique Guzman, a senior in high school and intern for 40th Ward Ald. Andre Vasquez, was also nominated to fill one of two required youth commissioner positions on the police oversight panel.</p><p>Guzman was raised by her grandparents after her father died when she was 2. Her uncle also died due to “cartel violence,” she said.</p><p>“I want to serve the people of this city by helping to ensure that our police department and our public safety systems represent the best ideas based on evidence, research and community interest,” Guzman said.</p>
entries.author: Mariah Woelfel | WBEZ

entries.title: Bulls introduce new man in charge as Bryson Graham hits all the notes
entries.summary: <p>Bryson Graham’s words on Wednesday were profound.</p><p>Then again, they often are in prepared introductory press conferences.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bulls" >Bulls</a> fans, however, are at the point where they deserve more than the decades-long distraction of the Michael Jordan statue in the United Center atrium representing their only recent positive history. They deserve more than words and promises from new hires.</p><p>Even one like Graham who has gone from interning with the New Orleans Pelicans back in 2010 in which his first day on the job involved unloading boxes at the practice facility, to now 16 years later having his boxes brought up into the Bulls’ swanky executive vice president of basketball operations office on the second floor of the Advocate Center.</p><p>Quite the impressive climb with stops at almost every rung on an NBA organization’s ladder.</p><p>But what’s needed now from this organization is someone that understands what the Bulls logo has meant to the city and what it means worldwide. More importantly, how lost it has become.</p><p>“If you played basketball in the (1990s), 85%, 90% of the kids, I don’t care where you grew up, I grew up in San Antonio — yeah, I got some love for the Spurs, I ain’t gonna lie, but it did not supplant what the Bulls meant to me,” Graham said. “When I think about it and I think about the greatness that’s in this building and what’s in these rafters, the championships that have been won here, it’s amazing. It impacted my life more than I thought I knew because I was always chasing what this organization represented.</p><p>“You see MJ, and obviously I wanted to be him just like everybody wanted, right. But I wanted that and it carried over in other aspects of my life, so like my work ethic, my determination, my grit, I’m watching the Bulls and what they displayed and I’m like, ‘That’s what I want.’ “</p><p>It’s now what he has.</p><p>Just one of the strengths that caught the eye of team president Michael Reinsdorf when it came down to making a final decision on who would replace fired exec Arturas Karnisovas.</p><p>Graham definitely didn’t short-cut his way to Chicago as was pointed out when he was introduced by Reinsdorf. An introduction that began with an apology.</p><p>“First I’ll start with our fans,” Reinsdorf said. “I want to, actually I need to, say I’m sorry because the results obviously haven’t been there. It’s not something I’m proud of and it’s something I want to get right. Ultimately it flows up to me and I take responsibility. I do feel that (Wednesday) is a step in the right direction and it’s an important step.</p><p>“At the end of the day, Bryson just clearly stood out. So why Bryson? I think it starts with he’s not about shortcuts.”</p><p>Good thing because he won’t be inheriting a quick fix. Graham knows that all too well making it very clear that they can’t miss on this upcoming 2026 draft class — especially since the Bulls have two picks in the top 15 of a talented group — they need to build out the front office to match the modern-day front offices around the league, and he also needs to find the right coach.</p><p>A bunch of pieces that turn into one collective force moving forward.</p><p>“I want a collaborative and very communicative organization,” Graham said. “It doesn’t matter about the position. Everyone in this room can have something that can add to making the right decision, so I want to take in what everyone says, and I want to process that.”</p><p>What he’s already processed? This organization needs to do whatever it takes to get back to hanging banners. That starts with the talent on the floor.</p><p>“Most rebuild situations is when you don’t have star-caliber players,” Graham said. “Right now, not to say we don’t have anyone on this roster that can get there, but until we continue to draft well, add to this mix, and add overall talent and team identity, yeah, we’re in a rebuilding phase.”</p><p></p><bsp-list-loadmore class="PageListH"
